paper.title: Foraging dynamics of Picasso's lifelong artistic creativity

sentences.text: In extreme cases, individuals dedicate their entire lives to continuous creative output.
Scientists, artists, writers, and other "hypercreative" individuals can spend decades in the sustained pursuit of creative breakthrough.
sentences.note: Elaborates [1]'s claim with the specific populations who exemplify it. Parent: [1] (emph).

sentences.text: This long-timescale creativity can be likened to a process of foraging, a strategic search for resources — in this case, creative ideas — in an uncertain landscape of possibility.
sentences.note: Introduces the foraging metaphor as the paper's core analytical construct.

sentences.text: Scientists, for instance, forage through a space of potential theories or mechanisms, while visual artists forage through a space of styles, themes, or techniques.
sentences.note: Illustrates the foraging metaphor from [3] with concrete domain examples, no citation. Parent: [3] (framedef).

sentences.text: Indeed, human creativity on a much shorter timescale — for instance, solving a puzzle over the course of seconds or minutes — has been characterized as a foraging process [1–4].
sentences.note: Citation-supported claim reporting prior research.

sentences.text: Much less is known about the dynamics of creativity over the long sweep of an entire career, particularly among those extreme, hypercreative individuals who devote their entire lives to a creative endeavor [5–7].
What strategies might characterize the long-timescale foraging dynamics of lifelong creativity?
sentences.note: Announces the guiding research question the study pursues.

sentences.text: A number of foraging strategies are likely candidates.
sentences.note: Pure structural announcement, no independent claim. Parent: [7] (contribution).

sentences.text: One that is widespread across living organisms is Area-Restricted Search [9], in which the foraging individual alternates between periods of widespread, global 'exploration' and periods of intensive, local 'exploitation' of the resources within a particularly resource-dense patch (e.g., seabirds foraging for fish [10]).
This alternation between exploration and exploitation has analogs in more abstract forms of search [11].
sentences.note: Citation-supported cross-domain generalization.

sentences.text: On the timescale of seconds and minutes, for instance, searching in semantic memory (e.g., for names of animals) often resembles Area-Restricted Search, with repeated cycles of exploitation (e.g., retrieving as many barnyard animals as possible) and exploration (i.e., searching for a new subtype of animal, e.g., pets) [2, 3].
sentences.note: Citation-backed domain-level pattern framed as an example; elevated per the rule that such sentences are tempstat even when illustrative in framing.

sentences.text: Similarly, when musicians improvise, they may trade off repeatedly between exploring widely in the space of possible sounds and exploiting a particular acoustic texture [12].
sentences.note: Citation-backed domain-level pattern, same basis as [11].

sentences.text: On the longer timescale of months and years, individual reading habits may follow an Area-Restricted Search strategy; indeed, computational analyses of Charles Darwin's reading habits found repeated alternations between exploitation (reading thematically related books) and exploration (switching to new topics) [13].
Thus, when humans are foraging for insights or information — whether internally in their memory or externally in books — they sometimes adopt a strategy of Area-Restricted Search.
sentences.note: Synthesizes the preceding sequence [11]–[13]. Parent: [13] (tempstat).

sentences.text: While Area-Restricted Search involves cyclical shifts between exploration and exploitation, long-timescale creativity might follow more directional patterns of change.
Long-timescale creativity, for instance, can involve progressive specialization.
sentences.note: Asserts the existence of a phenomenon (progressive specialization) as worth attending to.

sentences.text: Individuals might start with a vast exploration of diverse possibilities (e.g., random trial-and-error exploration of a large range of possibilities) before gradually honing in on a particular style, approach, practice, or idea.
sentences.note: Unpacks the specialization pattern asserted in [16]. Parent: [16] (emph).

sentences.text: We refer to this process of specialization as Constrictive Search.
sentences.note: Names the construct.

sentences.text: Constrictive Search might be especially common in domains where experts specialize in a narrow subset of all possible techniques or strategies.
sentences.note: Prediction without evidentiary support, per the worked example in the prompt. Parent: [18] (framedef).

sentences.text: Some scientists and artists, for instance, may begin their careers by searching widely across a large range of topics or styles before constricting to a particular area of expertise.
sentences.note: Illustrates [19], but since [19] is secondary, this re-parents to the nearest primary above, [18] (framedef), per the subordination rule rather than elevating [19].

sentences.text: Among scientists and artists who experience a 'hot streak' of sustained success [7], for instance, the hot streak is typically preceded by a period of widespread intellectual or stylistic exploration, followed by a constricted focus on the more narrow set of styles, themes, or topics that dominate their hot streak.
sentences.note: Citation-supported specific empirical finding.

sentences.text: More generally, constrictive search is the basis of simulated annealing in optimization [14] or transient diversity in collective problem solving [15, 16].
sentences.note: Citation-supported cross-domain generalization.

sentences.text: Here we consider whether Constrictive Search may even characterize entire careers.
sentences.note: Frames the specific question the present work examines.

sentences.text: The American painter Jackson Pollock, for instance, experimented with a variety of styles early in his career [17] before narrowing his output to his signature style of fractal 'drip' paintings [18].
sentences.note: Named individual used as an example with a primary parent ([23], contribution); stays illustrative even with citation, per the explicit rule. Parent: [23] (contribution).

sentences.text: A third potential foraging strategy in high-level creativity involves starting small — focusing on a limited set of styles, themes, or ideas — and expanding over time as a result of ongoing learning and adaptation.
sentences.note: Asserts the existence of a third foraging strategy pattern; not a bare topic label.

sentences.text: We refer to this as Expansive Search.
Formal models in ecology, for instance, have shown that organisms should increase their home-range — the region in which they forage — when resources become more scarce or random [19].
sentences.note: Citation-supported finding from a related domain, establishing the basis for the analogy.

sentences.text: This notion of an expanded home-range has analogs in creative domains.
sentences.note: Asserts that analogs exist (a claim of relevance/existence), not a bare topic label.

sentences.text: Mathematical discoveries, for instance, are scarce and difficult to foresee; mathematical experts gradually expand their toolbox, cultivating procedural flexibility that allows them to deploy a variety of strategies and techniques [20].

Visual artists, similarly, may incorporate new styles, themes, or techniques into their artistic practice, thus unlock new dimensions of possibility and qualitatively expanding the nature of the explored space itself.
sentences.note: Uncited parallel case extending [29]. Parent [29] is primary, so legal. Parent: [29] (tempstat).

sentences.text: Here, we investigate the foraging dynamics of artistic creativity of an especially prolific visual artist, Pablo Picasso.
Picasso is widely considered one of the most influential artists of the 20th century [21–23].

Picasso was so prolific that, between his first painting when he was 8 years old until he died at the age of 91, he produced on the order of 50000 works of art [23], averaging more than one new artwork every day of his entire life.
sentences.note: Citation-backed specific fact substantiating the "prolific" characterization rather than introducing a new generalization. Parent: [32] (tempstat).

sentences.text: Moreover, he's credited with contributing to a number of 20th century artistic developments, including Cubism and Surrealism [21–23].
sentences.note: Distinct citation-supported claim establishing significance (artistic movements).

sentences.text: As a complement to computational analyses that have zoomed out to identify visual or aesthetic patterns across many artists [24–28], we zoomed in on Picasso's prodigious output — hypercreative in both quantity and innovation — as an opportunity to investigate the dynamics of day-to-day creative foraging over the long sweep of an exceptionally creative career.
sentences.note: Positions the study's approach relative to prior work; states what the present work does.

sentences.text: We leveraged a digital catalogue of Picasso's prodigious output to characterize his long-timescale creative process.
We embedded images of every painting (N = 5008) into a high-dimensional 'style space' that captures both low-level and high-level stylistic features.
We then analyzed the trajectory within this style space of Picasso's day-to-day stylistic output over the course of his eight-decade career.

To characterize this trajectory, we calculated the magnitude of day-to-day stylistic movements (i.e., how much his style changed from day to day) and changes in the direction of this movement (i.e., the cosine similarity of successive movements).
We then investigate the long-timescale dynamics that emerge from this day-to-day stylistic foraging, including changes over his career in the scope of his stylistic output.
